[2025-02-01] U.S. Imposes Tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China; Canada Announces Retaliatory Tariffs
U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order on February 1, 2025, imposing new tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico, and China. The order mandates a 25% ad valorem duty on most Canadian goods and a 10% ad valorem duty on Canadian energy products, effective 12:01 a.m. ET on February 4, 2025. Trump cited the sustained influx of illicit drugs, including fentanyl, and illegal border crossings as reasons for the action, expanding a previously declared national emergency. The order invoked authorities under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the National Emergencies Act (NEA, specifying that duty-free de minimis treatment would not apply to affected articles. In response, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced 25% retaliatory tariffs against $155 billion worth of American goods. Canada's immediate response will target $30 billion worth of goods starting February 4, with an additional $125 billion in tariffs to follow in 21 days. Canadian officials disputed the U.S. claims regarding fentanyl and migrant origins. Premiers from several Canadian provinces expressed support for the federal government's retaliatory measures.

[2025-02-10] President Trump Orders Reinstatement of 25% Tariffs on Steel and Steel Derivatives, Increases Aluminum Tariffs to 25%, and Terminates Country Exemptions
President Donald J. Trump signed proclamations on February 10, 2025, reinstating 25% tariffs on all steel imports and increasing tariffs on aluminum imports from 10% to 25%. The actions, taken under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, terminated prior alternative agreements that had provided tariff relief to countries including Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, the European Union, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, Ukraine, and the United Kingdom. These countries will now be subject to the full 25% tariff.

Effective March 12, 2025, all steel, steel derivative products, and aluminum products previously covered by Section 232 will be subject to a 25% tariff. The proclamations also imposed an additional 25% ad valorem duty on certain new derivative steel and aluminum articles. The Commerce Secretary's authority to grant new product exclusion requests or renew existing ones was eliminated, effective immediately. A narrow exclusion was created for derivative steel and aluminum products originating from U.S.-melted or smelted articles. By May 11, 2025, the Commerce Secretary must establish a process for U.S. producers to request the inclusion of additional derivative products under these tariffs.

[2025-02-03] President Pauses Implementation of Tariffs on Mexican Goods Until March 4
The President on February 3, 2025, issued an order pausing the implementation of additional 25% ad valorem tariffs on products from Mexico. The administration cited Mexico's failure to intercept drug trafficking organizations, human traffickers, and illicit drugs as the reason for the tariffs, which were initially determined on February 1, 2025, under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The President declared the situation an "unusual and extraordinary threat" to U.S. national security, foreign policy, and economy. The pause extends the tariffs' effective date from February 4, 2025, to March 4, 2025, at 12:01 am eastern time. The order cited "immediate steps" taken by the Government of Mexico to address illegal migration and illicit drug crises. The Secretary of Homeland Security will assess the situation during this period, with the order stating tariffs could be immediately implemented if crises worsen or Mexico fails to take sufficient steps.

[2025-01-20] President Reinstates and Amends Schedule F, Renaming it Schedule Policy/Career, and Revokes EO 14003
On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump signed multiple executive orders on his first day in office. These actions included declaring a national emergency at the Mexico–United States border, blocking asylum seekers, and ending the "catch and release" policy for undocumented individuals. Executive Order 14165 reinstated the Migrant Protection Protocols ("Remain in Mexico"), terminated the CBP One mobile application, and ended categorical parole programs. An executive order directed the end of birthright citizenship for children born on or after February 19, 2025, to parents without lawful permanent residence or U.S. citizenship. The measure is facing a lawsuit. Refugee admissions were suspended, effective January 27, 2025, and international cartels were designated as terrorists. Executive Order 14159, titled 'Protecting the American People Against Invasion,' revoked previous Biden administration immigration proclamations and mandated actions on interior enforcement and detention. Separately, an executive order reinstated and amended Executive Order 13957, renaming "Schedule F" to "Schedule Policy/Career" within the federal workforce. This order stated that employees must faithfully implement administration policies, with failure constituting grounds for dismissal, and revoked Executive Order 14003. The Director of the Office of Personnel Management was directed to amend Civil Service Regulations. The President also initiated a review of the USMCA trade agreement and other trade agreements.

[2025-01-20] Trump Issues America First Trade Policy Memorandum Directing Review of Biden-Era Tech Policies
On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued several directives, including the America First Trade Policy memorandum. The memorandum instructed federal agencies to review technology policies enacted by the Biden administration.

Specific directives included the Commerce Secretary assessing U.S. intellectual property rights for Chinese persons and recommending reciprocal actions, and reviewing connected vehicle rulemaking for expanded controls. The Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the Commerce Secretary, was directed to review President Biden’s outbound investment executive order for potential modification or rescission and to assess existing national security controls. Agencies are required to submit these policy reviews by April 1, 2025.

Concurrently, President Trump issued a memorandum imposing a regulatory freeze. This directive ordered agencies not to propose or issue new rules until reviewed and approved by a Trump-appointed or designated department head. It also mandated the withdrawal of rules sent to the Office of the Federal Register but not yet published, for review and approval. This memorandum was published in the Federal Register on January 28, 2025 (90 FR 8249).

Additionally, President Trump issued a pardon proclamation for certain federal offenses related to the events at the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021. The proclamation allows eligible individuals to apply for a certificate of pardon through the Department of Justice after completing their sentences.

[2025-01-20] President Issues Memorandum Declaring OECD Global Tax Deal Has No Force or Effect in US
On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump delivered his second inaugural address, outlining plans for executive orders related to deportations, energy, and tariffs.

On the same day, the President issued a memorandum stating that prior administration commitments regarding the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Global Tax Deal have no force or effect in the United States unless adopted by Congress. The memorandum directed the Treasury Secretary and the U.S. Permanent Representative to the OECD to formally notify the organization of this position. It also instructed the Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the United States Trade Representative, to investigate foreign countries for non-compliance with tax treaties or for implementing extraterritorial tax rules on American companies, requiring a list of responsive actions within 60 days.

Additionally, the administration issued Executive Order 14154, “Unleashing American Energy,” directing federal agencies to immediately pause the disbursement of funds appropriated through the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. This has resulted in slowed funding for various programs and subsequent litigation.

[2025-01-20] President Trump Issues Executive Orders Reshaping Federal Environmental and Energy Policy on First Day of Second Term
On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump issued executive orders, memoranda, and proclamations. Actions included a presidential proclamation declaring an "invasion" at the southern border, suspending migrant entry and asylum requests. Executive Order 14165 "Securing Our Borders" reinstated the "Remain in Mexico" program, restarted border wall construction, and limited humanitarian parole. A national emergency was declared at the border, directing military deployment. An executive order also sought to reinterpret the 14th Amendment regarding birthright citizenship, facing immediate legal challenges.

In energy and environmental policy, Executive Order 14154 "Unleashing American Energy" promoted fossil fuel production and critical mineral development, and ended federal support for electric vehicles. The U.S. withdrew from the Paris Agreement and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change. A national energy emergency was declared to expedite fossil fuel projects.

Regarding federal operations, a hiring freeze was implemented across most executive branch agencies. A "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" was enacted for new rules. Federal employees were directed to return to in-person work. An executive order defined sex as an immutable biological classification, directing agencies to remove materials promoting "gender ideology." The administration also established an "America First Trade Policy" and revoked 58 executive orders and 10 presidential memoranda from the previous administration, targeting diversity, equity, and inclusion policies.

[2025-01-20] President Trump Signs Multiple Executive Actions on First Day in Office, Including Pardons and Birthright Citizenship Order, Prompting Legal Challenges
On January 20, 2025, his first day in office, President Donald Trump signed multiple executive actions. He pardoned approximately 1,500 individuals convicted in the Jan. 6, 2021 Capitol attack, commuted sentences for 14 others, and ordered an end to federal cases against his political opponents. Trump reinstated the "Remain in Mexico" policy, ended the CBP One app, and declared a national emergency at the southern border, also designating certain cartels as foreign terrorists. He issued an order aiming to end birthright citizenship and suspended the U.S. Refugee Admissions Program. In energy policy, Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Paris Climate Agreement, revoked several climate-related executive orders, declared a national energy emergency, and lifted the moratorium on new liquefied natural gas export licenses. He also temporarily withdrew areas on the Outer Continental Shelf from offshore wind leasing and began withdrawing the U.S. from the World Health Organization. Other actions included rescinding 78 Biden administration policies, implementing a federal hiring freeze, and issuing a proclamation recognizing only two sexes. These actions prompted immediate legal challenges.

[2025-01-31] President Trump Issues Executive Order 'Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation' to Reduce Federal Regulation
President Donald J. Trump issued an executive order titled "Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation" on January 31, 2025. The order mandates that federal agencies identify and repeal at least 10 existing rules, regulations, or guidance documents for every new one they issue. For fiscal year 2025, the order requires that the total incremental cost of all new regulations, including repealed ones, must be less than zero. The Director of the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) is tasked with standardizing the measurement and estimation of regulatory costs and implementing the order. Regulations concerning the military, national security, homeland security, foreign affairs, immigration, and agency organization/management/personnel are exempt. The order also revokes the 2023 update to OMB Circular A-4, reinstating the 2003 version, which changes discount rates to 3% and 7% and removes directives to consider effects outside the U.S. and emphasize distributional effects. Starting FY 2026, agencies must report regulatory costs/savings, and the OMB Director will set an incremental cost allowance for each agency.

[2025-02-01] US President Donald Trump Signs Orders Imposing Tariffs on Canada and Mexico
On February 1, 2025, U.S. President Donald Trump signed orders imposing tariffs on goods imported from Canada and Mexico. The orders mandated 25% tariffs on most imports from both countries, with a 10% tariff specifically on oil and energy imports from Canada. In response, then-Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced Canada's intention to impose 25% tariffs on CA$30 billion (US$20.6 billion) of American goods, with plans to increase to CA$155 billion (US$106 billion) after three weeks. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum also stated Mexico would implement retaliatory tariffs and non-tariff measures. A one-month delay for the tariffs was negotiated on February 3. The U.S. tariffs took effect on March 4, with Canada's retaliatory tariffs beginning simultaneously. Mexico stated it would delay its response. On March 6, Trump exempted goods compliant with the United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA) from the tariffs.
Key facts:

[2025-01-20] Trump Issues Executive Orders for Federal Employee Hiring Freeze and Return to In-Person Work
President Trump, following his inauguration on January 20, 2025, issued a series of executive actions. He directed the Secretary of State to align U.S. foreign policy with an "America First" directive, prioritizing American interests and citizens. On immigration, actions included "realigning" the Refugee Admission Program, designating cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, declaring a national emergency at the southern border, and directing construction of a border wall. The administration also moved to end "catch and release," reinstate "Remain in Mexico," and suspend refugee resettlement from January 27.

Regarding federal employees, Trump mandated all executive branch personnel return to in-person, full-time work, ending remote arrangements. He also imposed a 90-day hiring freeze on federal civilian employees, effective January 20, 2025, for unfilled positions, with exemptions for national security and immigration enforcement. The freeze excludes military personnel and prohibits using third-party contractors to bypass it. Additionally, Trump initiated actions to withdraw the U.S. from the Paris Agreement, declared a national energy emergency, and temporarily halted new wind energy projects on federal lands and waters. These orders also paused funding from the Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.

[2025-01-20] President Trump Signs Executive Orders on Energy Policy, Declares National Emergency, Withdraws from Paris Agreement, and Pauses Offshore Wind Leasing
On January 20, 2025, President Trump signed multiple executive actions. Executive Order 14165, titled "Securing Our Borders," mandated physical barriers, increased border personnel, expanded detention facilities, and reinstated "Remain in Mexico" policies. The order also called for ceasing CBP One use, terminating parole programs, and increasing prosecutions for border-related crimes.

Separately, Trump issued executive orders on energy policy. He declared a national energy emergency, citing high prices, which enabled powers like the Defense Production Act. Other orders reduced regulatory burdens, lifted the moratorium on new liquefied natural gas export licenses, and addressed Alaska's natural resource utilization. The administration also directed the U.S. withdrawal from the Paris Climate Agreement and temporarily withdrew Outer Continental Shelf areas from offshore wind leasing.

A Presidential Memorandum initiated a hiring freeze for federal civilian employees across the executive branch, prohibiting the filling of vacant or new positions. Exemptions included military, immigration enforcement, national security, and public safety roles. The freeze was initially set to expire for most agencies within 90 days, pending an Office of Management and Budget (OMB) workforce reduction plan, but was later extended.

[2025-02-01] President Trump Imposes Additional Tariffs on China, Pauses Tariffs on Canada and Mexico
On February 1, 2025, President Donald J. Trump signed three executive orders imposing additional tariffs on products from Canada, Mexico, and China. The order for Canada included an additional 25% tariff on most products and 10% on energy resources. Tariffs on Mexico included an additional 25% on all products, while tariffs on China's products were set at an additional 10%. The tariffs on China went into effect on February 4, 2025. The tariffs on Canada and Mexico, also scheduled for February 4, 2025, were suspended for one month after phone calls between President Trump and the leaders of those two countries. The tariffs are in addition to existing tariffs, do not include an exclusion process, and are imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEPPA).

[2025-01-20] President Issues Memorandum Ordering Regulatory Freeze Pending Review for Executive Departments and Agencies
On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued a memorandum titled "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" to the heads of executive departments and agencies. The directive ordered an immediate halt to proposing or issuing new rules without approval from department or agency heads appointed after January 20, 2025. Agencies were instructed to withdraw any rules sent to the Office of the Federal Register (OFR) but not yet published. The memorandum also directed agencies to consider postponing for 60 days the effective date of rules already published or issued but not yet in effect, to allow for review of factual, legal, and policy questions. The freeze applies to 'rules' as defined by the Administrative Procedure Act and Executive Order 12866. Exceptions are granted for emergency situations or rules subject to statutory or judicial deadlines, with the Director or Acting Director of the Office of Management and Budget authorized to grant such exemptions and oversee implementation. Similar regulatory freezes have been issued by previous administrations.

[2025-01-20] Donald Trump Signs Record 26 Executive Orders on First Day of Second Presidency
Donald Trump began his second term as President of the United States on January 20, 2025, signing 26 executive orders. On that day, he declared a "national energy emergency" and initiated the United States' withdrawal from the 2015 Paris Climate Agreement. He signed Executive Order 14154, "Unleashing American Energy," directing federal agencies to revise regulations on domestic energy resources and revoking the American Climate Corps.

Other initial actions included initiating withdrawal from the World Health Organization, revising federal recognition of gender identity, establishing the Department of Government Efficiency, pardoning over 1,500 individuals involved in the January 6 Capitol riot, designating Mexican drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, and declaring a national emergency at the southern border.

The administration also announced cabinet and cabinet-level nominations, including Marco Rubio for Secretary of State and Pamela Bondi for Attorney General. Individuals with backgrounds as oil, gas, and chemical lobbyists were appointed to the Environmental Protection Agency. Some of these orders have been blocked in court.
